author: @JoeHacobian
text:
I’ve gone solar, but I’m smart enough to know it’s not sufficient for:

Large scale desalination
Re-greening of deserts
Direct de-carbonization of oceans for jet fuel synthesis and closing the aviation fuel loop.

To re-irrigate desertification and turn parched valleys into green pastoral land you need nuclear power.

To produce nitrogen for fertilizer in order to feed the world (eradicate world hunger) you need nuclear power.

Sure there’s a place for solar and wind and that place will look better once credible energy storage is solved but there’s no getting around the fact that healing the planet will require nuclear power. Fission for the next century or two and phasing from fission to fusion during the 2100’s. The 21st century is a fission century and it should be a thorium powered one in order to cut down on the long lived wastes.

By the time we humans master fusion, we will have learned how to safely burn up that 93% un-utilized nuclear fuel we call ‘waste’.

author: @JoeHacobian

That’s enough time for fusion to finally get sorted out.

replies.author: @MrSec84

Where did you get this idea?
Every part of a solar panel can be recycled with current techniques, they're literally made of a coating of either glass or clear plastic, conductive metal, silicon (which is essentially sand like the glass) and a solid or stable backing.
All materials we have refined processes to handle in recycling right now.

Also bare in mind Solar can now be manufactured at a production cost of $8 USD per square meter, using a conventional printer used for making product labels, an Australian company is doing it right now, so cost of the technology is going to filter to customers soon to make it very feasible for anyone to install on their house's roof.

Really the only issue is storage and considering Robert already has a video talking about how cheap and usable Sand Batteries are it's possible to go completely solar and renewable, off grid for some work and not a lot of money.

replies.author: @MrSec84
replies.text:
The Finnish Polar Night Energy has basically solved this problem with their Sand Battery, they heat the sand up to 500c, underground and the medium can hold that heat for months in times of extreme winter.

The sand storage has a metal pipe system running through it that can pass water to be boiled for instant heating in already made pipes within their towns and cities.

If you were building a system at home you could easily just make a hole in the ground or factor it in as a part of a new foundation system, construction is very cheap and pretty simple.

For energy you would also just incorporate a Peltier Thermoelectric converter (small versions can be bought from Amazon and eBay to test out).

With versions of Solar like the cheap Printed stuff in Australia and also using well considered insulation it should be very easy to solve all of our power and heating issues for not a lot of money using the tech we have available.

replies.author: @mxguy2438
replies.text: @MrSec84 not holding my breath on sand storage. I wish it luck but I'm guessing it performs best with larger size which pretty much rules it out for home storage, particularly when you consider the efficiency lost in the conversation back to electricity. It might be a good way of heating apartment buildings in a city in the north. I don't see it being adopted on a large scale.

replies.author: @MrSec84
replies.text:
@mxguy2438 check out Robert's video titled "1607 A DIY Sand Battery" in it Robert shows that a basic compact metal tank (with no additional insulation), filled with 60KGs of Sand, sat on the side of a brick wall could be heated up to over 440 degrees C in an hour with nothing but some pine burning, Rob got tired waiting around for it to cool down to ambient temperatures.

The sand will just hold the heat for long periods of time, buried underground or with some actual kind of insulation around the storage vessel the heat will obviously stay for longer contained.
Given that it's just sand it's cheap or any freely available medium could be next to nothing to get.

Pair this with solar or some other renewable and you'll just be topping up the heat faster than you can use it.

Point is a pretty compact version would do the job for even home use.
Yes larger versions will store heat for longer, but that isn't necessary to make something yourself for cheap.

You could easily have something like a hot water tank that you'd have in your home, just filled with sand, insulated well it would hold heat for ages and be very cheap to make.

author: @danielpanywhere
text:
I personally still think that by the end of the day, the main bottle-neck will be the aging, unimproved electrical infrastructure (i.e. the power company), at least in the U.S.

On one hand, the advancement of human-kind is generally measured by the amount of energy we demand, so we should be proud to consume not only this much energy, but exponentially more as time goes on. Simultaneously, we are responsible as good stewards to learn how to get 100% of our energy without consuming, damaging, or polluting our planet.

The 'anti-alternative' argument doesn't really make sense when you take a step back and think about it. People are virtually making the point that if we can't fix everything immediately, we should just give up and keep making things worse. It took us over 200 years to get into the problem in which we find ourselves, early industrial pollution included, and we probably won't have the solution this afternoon.

If it turned out PV solar panels couldn't practically power the entire world, even with coming improvements that will make them several times more efficient, we could also consider that a single molten salt solar tower in the desert can provide nearly 20MW of continuous electricity during the day, or can store up to 1,100MWh for nighttime use by up to 75,000 homes (source: New Concentrating Solar Tower Is Worth Its Salt with 24/7 Power. Scientific American, July 14, 2016). Several of these type of stations have previously been built in the American southwest deserts over the past 30 years, some sponsored entirely by the National Renewable Energy Laboratory (NREL), and each version is an improvement over the last.

This is not to even mention the literally hundreds of other power generation techniques being improved upon in the fields of Permanent Magnets, Differential Temperature, Wind, Water, Geo-Thermal, Gravity, Radio / Induction, Energy Storage, etc.

replies.text:
Amazon. You can get panels on there for good value. Expect to pay a bit under £1/W. Getting the panels isn't your only problem though: There is a lot of regulation around UK wiring, and all of it is built on the (usually true) assumption that most home-owners shouldn't be trusted to hold a screwdriver. If you want to go with grid tied, you'll need to inform the DNO - if you don't then your smart-meter will alert them anyway, and they will be very unhappy indeed if your installation isn't signed off by a certified professional and constructed using only appropriately certified equipment with the paperwork to prove it. It's easier if you are aiming for full self-sufficiency because you will be able to simply ignore the DNO, and trust that no-one cares enough about your blatantly criminal installation. Though if there is ever a fire, even one that has nothing to do with the electrics at all, the home insurance company may still seize upon the excuse to deny cover.

Unless you are a professional electrician yourself, there is no legal way to DIY solar. So decide first of all: Are you happy to ignore the law? If you are, then get bodging: It'll be a fraction of the cost!

I had hoped to do the same, but my house is tall and narrow, the roof is small and very hard to access.

replies.text:
​@ThinkingandTinkering ​ I totally agree that the energy can be buffered with existing storage technologies such as: pumped hydro, stationary batteries, hydrogen, maybe even thermal storage like the Finns. But these options are either expensive or relatively low efficiency, which effectively increases the cost of the power, to the point that if it isn't used at point of generation then it might still be cheaper to buy fossil fuel power which can be varied on demand.  

I just thought it was funny you put all the emphasis on if solar could fulfil the worlds energy requirements, when I thought that had been established long ago. 

The issue was cost, and is now the duck curve. We don't currently have the energy storage capacity to deal with the dips. Hence stories like sand thermal energy storage getting loads of attention because they can be part of the solution. And how people still talk about Nuclear for "base load".

Ultimately, the biggest problem was that it was more profitable to burn fossil fuels than use renewables until relatively recently. Now that renewable is cheapest, the transition is picking up speed. Storage is getting cheaper too. Once stored solar/wind is cheaper than non renewable options, it'll all transition over. 

Currently though, gas generated electricity is still cheaper per kWh than losing half of your power in a cheap inefficient storage system, or an efficient but expensive one. 

Technical feasibility hasn't been what has been holding us back. It's as much economics as engineering. We need to engineer cheaper storage solutions in order for stored renewable energy to be cheaper than fossil fuel. Then it will be done. 

Because the world is largely capitalist, and profits take priority, driving behaviour.

author: @Berkana
text: Robert, with regards to where solar panels are placed, there is an enormous opportunity to mitigate one problem while solving another by co-locating solar panels over parking lots and warehouses and other structures that would otherwise need to be airconditioned. replies.text: Not really. Wind doesn't scale down well. It's in the physics - power output is proportional to the cross sectional area and to the cube of the wind speed. The wind speed goes up with height. So wind turbines are very practical if you build them /big/. There is a good reason that wind farms use a small number of gigantic turbines rather than a lot of small ones. Wind for domestic installation just doesn't provide enough power to be worth it. Solar does. Also, small wind turbines have to spin faster, which means lots more noise.

author: @didierdelay6623
text: A question troubles me for a while now: How much energy is "lost" by transporting electricity from the power station down to the consumer? I mean those 100s sometimes 1000s Km of cable must act as some kind of resistor, them transform a part of the electricity into heat which is totally wasted.

replies.author: @rotulaelectrica
replies.text: You are right in a way. It is wasted. We just have no other more efficient means to transport huge amount of energy from nuclear, coal, hydro etc power stations up to the consumers. A transformer can go up to 99.99% efficiency. Amazing. Now, the energy consumed in a year by a 400MVA having 99.99% efficiency is roughly 35GWh. Huge! Still, we need those transformers. The trouble is that if we want to make the transformer 99.999% efficient we could go down to 3,5GWh consumed by the transformer in order to function, which would be great. On the other hand, it would become prohibitively expensive to produce so we just settle to that 99.99%. Same goes for power lines.

If you look at everything we need to do to solve climate change, I don't think energy storage is such a big issue. We have to replace fossil fuels for all our transportation with batteries and hydrogen, and we must replace gas/coal with hydrogen for fertilizer, chemicals and steel production too. This will give us a *massive* capacity to do load balancing. We could actually set aside some hydrogen being produced and feed it into the gas power plants we already have today, for days with very little sun and wind.
We're going to produce batteries on the same scale needed to shift energy from day to night time anyway. My EV can power the night-time consumption of our house for many days.

author: @no1be4me2
text: The concept of net energy must be applied to renewable sources of energy, such as windmills and photovoltaics. A two-megawatt windmill contains 260 tonnes of steel requiring 170 tonnes of coking coal and 300 tonnes of iron ore, all mined, transported and produced by hydrocarbons. The question is: how long must a windmill generate energy before it creates more energy than it took to build it? At a good wind site, the energy payback day could be in three years or less; in a poor location, energy payback may be never. That is, a windmill could spin until it falls apart and never generate as much energy as was invested in building it.

replies.text:
Standard solar panels are 1.6 m2 for 300 Watts. So 15 m2 means about 3 Kw installed power.
Rule of thumb here in the Netherlands Kwh/yr = Watts installed.
3 Kw installed = 3000 Kwh/yr. That makes 8 Kwh/day for 15 m2  not 15!

My 1.8 Kw system runs for 10 years now and has delivered 18.000 Kwh so far in total so our rule of thumb seems rather accurate while yours is quite optimistic.

replies.author: @simonpeggboard4004
replies.text:
@janhemmer8181 Solar panels vary in size but the Average panel size now= 1.8m2 with
Peak Panel Power= 360W.
= 200w/m2 (same as 20% efficiency with Sun Power= 1Kw/m2).

Avg 8hrs sun per day.
Avg 265 Sun Days per year.
= 2120 Hours Sunlight Per year.

2120@200w/m2 = 424KWh/year.

=1.162 Kw/Day/m2
Allowing for inverter, cable and other incidental losses gives the approximate 1Kw/m2 value.

As with all things Thumbs vary in size, Maybe mine is just bigger than yours.

replies.author: @newmonengineering
replies.text: @rogerstarkey5390 look at it this way, if you have a controller that is designed to run at say 100v and you have 4 panels in series. If you lose 25% that is 1 whole panel on that circuit. It will not meet the minimum voltage to charge at some point. You will have to have a different controller just to keep ot going. Controllers on the huge scale may be in the thousands of volts. Once it drops to some number the controller doesn't function because you no longer satisfy the minimum cut off voltage to keep the system operating. It may not charge until its at say 80% of the voltage. These things have designed cut off voltages where if it's below the voltage it cannot charge. Also you can get imbalances in the arrays. At some point you lose the entire string because it's not productive at its designed voltage range. It's like if you have 10v panel and you want to charge a 12v battery you need to fix the circuit to get more than 13v before it functions properly. Sure it may still produce some voltage but if it's not enough,  it doesn't matter it's useless. So you have to add more constantly and monitor and replace them to keep the arrays up to par.

author: @ThisRandomUsername
text:
If everyone looked into personal solar power and realised how much energy they actually use, I feel like a lot of wastage would stop happening.
The biggest problem with solar is always going to be storage. Just Have A Think has just done  a video on thermal storage, and I believe a domesticated version of that is probably the answer. I'm going to be building a ~2000l hot water storage tank like David Poz did a few years ago as my next major project. You could do the same for cold water storage too, just less densely.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I was thinking of compressed air - but I like the idea of thermal

replies.author: @ThisRandomUsername
replies.text:
​@ThinkingandTinkering 
Thermal is pretty safe for a hobbyist to attempt. Compressed air is a good idea if you can get a large pressure vessel capable of very high pressures. It looks like you get industrial tanks at ~5000l capable of ~10 bar which really isn't much energy.
The theoretical energy (joules) you could store in that size tank would be:
work = PbVb x ln (Pa/Pb) + (Pb - Pa)Vb
= [1MPa x 5m^3 x ln(0.1/1)] + [(1 - 0.1) x 5m^3]
= [-11,5] + [4.5]
= -7MJ
= -1.9kWh

Comparatively, storing energy in water as heat is much easier:
Thermal energy = mass x (Tb - Ta) x specific heat capacity
 = 2000kg x (70C - 45C) x 4kJ/kgK
 = 200 MJ
 = 55kWh

Formula for compressed air energy storage is from Wikipedia Isothermal example.

Ever wondered what will happen to the climate if we keep building wind farms.  Wind is generated by the air moving from areas of high to low pressure and the rate it moves affects our weather. Is there a limit of how much energy we can extract before we notice the lows are taking longer to dissipate and the rain or dry spells are lasting for weeks and not days.

 I found when flying that the wind direction changes as you get lower to the ground due to it being dragged about by the obstacles rotating with the earth. So by adding metal and plastic trees are we also going to make to shift direction more? 

So much to think about with my poor old gray cells!

author: @Kalanchoe1
text: Although i love solar power for a variety of reasons, there are still many other barriers solar preventing them from being an easy world solution. If you generate all the worlds’ power in one location the problem is power transmission and distribution. We need power at night time when the sun doesn’t shine which means we need to be able to store excess solar at scale which is beyond our means cost-wise. Solar panels only get peak efficiency at certain angles to the sun, so stationary panel's efficiency varies throughout the day. Some of these things don’t matter as much in desert like climates. But then solar panel efficiency and lifespan drops with increasing running temperature. Colder climates use more power during the winter when the sun is much weaker or practically non-existent. 1kW per square meter is lovely when you live at the equator with noon-day sun. You can make up missing power with more panels but it becomes costly quickly. The reason we love turbine related power generation like nuclear and hydropower and coal is that their output powers can be variable when peak power generation is required which tends to be supper time and mornings. The quickest variability being with nuclear and hydropower. Hydropower is geographically limited. Uranium fuel isn’t the only nuclear fuel we can use but gets little attention because nuclear=bad for reasons. Where solar is viable, it should definitely be used as an adjunct to power generation.

replies.author: @rogerstarkey5390
replies.text:
I have a few issues with your assumptions.
1) Why would you generate all the world's power in one place? That was simply an example.
In fact, as Robert suggested, the sensible way would be to essentially "fragment" the system, moving generation as close a possible to "point of use, for example rooftops, *or* walls in more northern latitudes, (ref the Copenhagen International School, provides 50% of its annual energy from "wall solar")
As for "power at night time".... Most buildings use less power at night. Providing low level storage at the location will satisfy much of the demand.
.
2) Winter use? (Ref Copenhagen)
.
3) "Efficiency".
The trick would be to not necessarily concentrate on outright efficiency, but cist per square metre, then utilise the maximum effective area.
How about an office building? Say 20+ floors with a South facing wall.
We have new transparent thin film solar becoming available.
No as efficient (yet) but such a film on windows would collect more than sufficient from an oblique angle in summer, be idea for the low sun in winter, and could be used to effectively cool in summer (it's removing energy!)
You actually referenced another opportunity, passive solar. If "panel efficiency decreases with temperature", build in a heat collection circuit and use the heat energy.
.
4) It doesn't have to be ALL solar.
For instance, Robert cited "5MW onshore wind turbines".
The latest offshore units are 16MW.
For example
We (UK) have the Dogger Bank project due for completion in 2025/26, coincidentally almost the same time as the comparable Hinkley C Nuclear project.
.
Dogger Bank will:-
Take about 7 years less time (including consultation)
.
Cost £8.7bn compared to a now projected £23+bn for Hinkley.
.
Ramp in stages, reducing the carbon footprint of "remaining fossil generation" over the the build period.
.
Have an output of 3.6 GW compared to 3.2 GW.
.
Provide energy at a strike price of around £39/MW, compared to £92/MW (!)
.
Have a much faster return on investment, thus being more attractive to independent investors.
.
Have built in resilience, consisting of numerous turbines. If one fails, you lose a tiny percentage, not the whole output.
.
5) "Nuclear bad"
You cite the possibility of "other fuel types".
GREAT!
When?
As far as I'm aware, the "small modular Thorium reactors" are in early prototype stage and the first (China?) may be ready for test in 3 years.
.
Testing? Add 4 years?
.
Then, the first production units, with a VERY optimistic 4-5 year build time? (They're "small" reactors?)
.
Will the country which builds the first successful prototype "donate" the design for the good if the planet?
.
If so, will other countries (you know who) accept if they did?
Will there be political pressure not to give the technology to *everyone* (those regimes "out of favour")?
.
To be effective, it MUST be used by everyone.
.
It will be a nightmare.
.
Good luck getting any agreement in 5-10 years from the date of any offer.
.
You can of course add the inevitable "FUD" and delaying tactics from "other interested parties" (if you think the EV FUD is bad, just wait)
When the *realistic* lead time including consultation, "NIMBYism", etc is approaching 2 decades, (ref Hinkley C) how will that be reduced?
.
See the problem?
.
If you have a plan that gets the "new nuclear" build project up and running, globally(!) In 10 years, GREAT! (I won't hold my breath)

replies.author: @Kalanchoe1
replies.text:
@rogerstarkey5390 Generating the world power in one spot is stupid and doesn’t serve as a useful point for anything.

Of course a car battery could run all my house lighting needs at night but what are you heating your house with? Thermo pump/oil hybrid is the bulk of my energy cost during winter. I would love to see that work on solar even though I am at lower latitude (10ish degrees) than Copenhagen our average yearly low is 4c lower with 10c lower record lows. If you think Copenhagen has a cold winter. Over 2x population of Copenhagen in my city alone, a lot of people here heat electric only. Ain’t any small storage going to heat our houses at night. 
And I’m not even cherry picking my example.

Stick as many of the cheapest solar panels you can fit on the south side of a building, hope no other buildings cast shadows and let me know how much useful energy you get out of the limited solar irradiance you got.
The limited lifespan of solar cells, difficulty of recycling and the environmental nightmare that is refining silicon makes a hard argument for powering the world. Especially when a pandemic is enough to cause a silicon shortage in the supply chain.

You seem to be optimistic about low energy harvesting techniques. Trying to convert small thermal differentials into electricity never works well. Thermo-electric generators are effectively peltier devices with already poor efficiency. This case comes down to fighting entropy; there bulk energy in the house but it costs too much energy to concentrate it. Likewise, a positive thermal difference is require for this to work as passive “cooling” to which you can never go below ambient. Just open a window instead. (I’ve seen this idea before as a proof of concept; they got like 50mW out of a square meter)
“transparent thin film solar” implies transmitting the entire visible spectrum and therefore losing half the available energy compared to conventional solar (ignoring efficiency). This sounds expensive to manufacture and limited in a practical sense. Since sunlight hitting at a shallow angle innately has little energy to harvest in the first place. Just use low emissivity glass or a double pane and save on your homes heating/cooling.

Solar has real advantages and limitations to acknowledge, it’s not the end all be all.
 I know it doesn’t need to all solar but wasn’t that the pitch of the video title?

The case for nuclear lies in thorium and uranium power "since breeder reactors effectively recycle most of their waste (nuclear waste)" wikipedia. Fast breeder reactors have been proven to work and even have commercial prototypes. India will not stop working on thorium plants since they are the largest source of thorium and want to be power independent. India works on cleaner solar tech too but we will see how long it will take them to bring it to mass production. The nuclear tech is there and real. Just has unwarranted fear around it.
“Research and development of thorium-based nuclear reactors, primarily the liquid fluoride thorium reactor (LFTR), MSR design, has been or is now being done in the United States, United Kingdom, Germany, Brazil, India, Indonesia, China, France, the Czech Republic, Japan, Russia, Canada, Israel, Denmark and the Netherlands.” Wikipedia. It seems like thorium plants may very well be accessible to many countries especially when Canada and the US has worked with china on the tech while Canada has plans to run plants in Chile and Indonesia.

author: @ProlificInvention
text: I once saw a video for a motor built using Nitinol wires that utilized the temperature differential of some hot and cold water.  It was quite interesting, and when I was commenting on it on another of your videos someone had mentioned it's specifications and that it made over a few horsepower.  My idea is to make a system that heats water with infrastructure under solar panels that absorb the heat, said water is pumped out when to the proper temperature into insulated storage tanks.  During the nighttime hours or high demand times the hot water is used to run nitinol motor powered generators.   As far as solar I think the ultimate solution is in a video I made called "My best invention: Solar power line conversions" which is an idea for flexible solar cell rolls to be bent in the middle like a little peaked roof and attached to power lines.  I would treat the solar cell surfaces to make them super hydrophobic and oleophobic thereby keeping them free of animal excitement and water droplets/ice formation resistance.  The math shows millions of miles of power lines would have the capacity to generate all our power needs, and the infrastructure where it is needed would be modified to utilize it.

replies.author: @colleenforrest7936
replies.text:
Interesting idea. Now you have to think about the infustructure.  This is not a criticism but part of your to-do list.  

Are these cable hats going to be delivered to the installation site already attached to the cable or attached separately over the cable.   

If on the cable, cables are stored on spools.  How much less footage of cable will be able to be wound around a cable with the hat installed.

If you are adding to a cable after installation, is how much additional time will the installer spend installing the hats?  Also, power cables have a residual energy in them (not so much in cable or phone wires) you are going to have to ground these.  (Actually, could you retrieve energy from the wire as well)

Then there's weather.  How much heavier would this hatted cable after an ice or snow storm?  Will they be more susceptible to wind?

Again, not a criticism, just exciting problems to solve.

replies.author: @ProlificInvention
replies.text: @colleenforrest7936 Absolutely, thank you for commenting, made my day.  What gave me the idea was there are plastic things that I described exactly on the power lines near my home, the just aren't flexible solar cell material.  They've been up there for 10 years plus here in Michigan and one did fall down which I found on my walk and they appear to be held on with plastic zip ties or the like, just a few slots in the heavy vinyl like plastic.  My idea was to retrofit existing lines as a neighbor told me they put those plastic things up years ago for which purpose he did not know, but at the time I was experimenting with what at the time was new technology flexible solar cells that cone on a roll, they were very expensive was their only drawback-but being plastic they were ultra durable and very similar to the material that's already on the lines near my home. author: @raymondglad5593
text: Solar can make a big difference to household bills. Its a question of management. Run straight of 48v dc fridge or ac. Store the energy as ice to use at night. No battery and no inverters. Dc lighting from small battery and use large appliances like washing machines during the day.
